labor_efficiency_multipliers.explanation: The robot works 2.69 times more hours per day (21.5 vs 8). Because it compresses its work into a shorter lifespan, its lifetime labor density is 4.14 times higher relative to its total time on Earth.

energy_efficiency_gains.explanation: The robot's power source is 3.5 times more efficient at delivery than the human food chain. When accounting for full lifetimes, the robot requires 7.74 times less total energy from the planet for every hour of work it delivers.

economic_efficiency_gains.explanation: It costs 10.1 times less to manufacture the robot than to raise a human. Factoring in fuel, production, and human salary, the robot operates at a total burdened cost savings factor of 109.32 times cheaper per work hour.

manufacturing_feedback_loops.explanation: When humanoid robots build copies of themselves, the loop self-accelerates. Because robots work 2.69 times longer shifts than humans and can operate in pairs or doubles without scheduling friction, the active labor velocity inside the factory doubles. A robot workforce cuts its own 4-week manufacturing timeline down to under a week of real-world labor time per unit.

compounding_workforce_scaling_ratios.explanation: Human generations take 18 to 25 years to reproduce and scale labor capacity. A robot factory run entirely by robots can replicate its entire workforce footprint every 4 weeks. With multi-line parallel assembly, output is limited only by raw material delivery, scaling workforce capacity exponentially rather than biologically.

macro_industrial_flywheel.explanation: A deployed robot needs only 1.86 days of active 21.5-hour work to generate the 15,000 MJ of energy it took to build it. Financially, it saves its own $30,000 creation cost in 1.25 days of work when compared to the burdened cost of equivalent human labor slots.
